body.title: Architecture-as-code / cognitive coding language — whys as the lever, cognition pipeline as the spine
body.text:
# D-architecture-as-code-cognition-pipeline-2026-06-16
**title:** Architecture-as-code / cognitive coding language — whys as the lever, cognition pipeline as the spine
**date:** 2026-06-16
**status:** open
**spawned_tickets:** T-why-sorter, T-per-ticket-checkpoint, T-consequence-architecture-as-code-cognition-pipeline

## Hypothesis
When a structure has no recorded why, the system either captures one (asking Akien or a free cloud LLM) or flags it as debris; and a mid-ticket compaction no longer strands uncommitted work, because work-state is checkpointed per-ticket.

## Measurement Signal
Logs show fewer loose-file incidents after compaction, and why-capture/debris-flag signals appear on rationale-less constraints.

## Goal Link
Serves the self-improvement goal (system self-improvement is a primary goal) — closing the observe→learn→improve loop and the External State Principle.

## Decision narrative
A multi-round Opus design conversation on "Architecture as code." Core spine captured as NARRATIVE (not all buildable yet); two pieces extracted as buildable tickets plus a consequence check.

**The whys are the lever.** Store mechanism / how-it-works, not outputs — mechanism is what lets you rebuild things in new ways. This makes the system a *compiler*, not a *cache*. `constraint_normalizer.py` is the embryo of architecture-as-code (normalizes prose rules → queryable `devlab.constraints`). The why-sorter ticket extends it: capture missing rationale, or flag its absence as debris.

**Architecture-as-code = a two-axis intent graph.** Horizontal: peer node →meets-need→ node. Vertical: user intent → architectural intent → patterns/constraints → plan/sequence → code, each level "in service of the level above."

**One graph, many projections.** The sequence diagram = Intent Doc (BDD Given/When/Then) = build plan = test scenario = arch graph — dynamic projections of the same intent graph. The schema/IR is the artifact; a "generalized sequence designer" rides on top.

**"Build this" decomposes into a cognition pipeline:** orient → examine intentions → check constraints → assess/judge → plan → build. The bus devices already shipped (embedding agent, intention extractor, judge, librarian/orientation, codebase-knower) ARE those steps disassembled — the SAME pipeline as the test factory (intent-extractor → constraint-normalizer → scenario-generator → judge). Planning is the next step to move cloud→trees, and planning's artifact IS the sequence/intent graph.

**The cognitive coding language is the IR** that makes incremental cloud→trees migration possible — a step can only be swapped behind a stable interface. Grow it from real adjacent seams (intent-extractor→planner first); Intent Doc is the first fragment. Do NOT bless a grand schema top-down. DISCIPLINE for "generalize once it works": name the seams in cognition terms now (intent→structured-intent) even while implementations are coding-specific, or generalization later becomes a rewrite.

**Token strategy (three-tier):** free cloud LLM = orientation/breadth ("where to look"); SWADL motor cortex (compiled, reusable; GoogleSecretary already a SWADL user) = actuator; librarian = orchestrator; expensive brain = judgment only. Guardrail: free LLMs on "where to look," NEVER "is this right."

**Product reframe:** the system compiles EXPERT JUDGMENT made reusable, not manufactured judgment — non-programmers get architecturally-consistent (drift-checked) code before architecturally-original code. "Which patterns and why" is captured FROM Akien (the system can ask him, or ask free cloud LLMs).

**Compaction = architecture problem.** Mid-work compaction leaves loose files because work-state lives in context, not a durable store — the External State Principle, the same problem architecture-as-code solves. → per-ticket-checkpoint ticket.

### Mechanism revision (2026-06-16, Akien design session — supersedes the checkpoint-file plan for T-per-ticket-checkpoint)
The per-ticket-checkpoint ticket was re-shaped from "durable checkpoint file + selective preserve string + summarize" to a **clear-to-base** primitive, after claude-code-guide research established what Claude Code natively supports:
- **`/rewind` → "Restore conversation"** rewinds the *conversation* to a chosen prompt **while keeping current code** — and checkpointing tracks only Edit/Write tool edits, **never bash `git commit`**, so commits are automatically safe. This is exactly "clear back to base, keep the work."
- **`/compact` is a lossy AI summary, NOT clean truncation** — so summarize-and-rehydrate was the wrong primitive.
- **Programmatic transcript (JSONL) truncation is unsupported** — do not hand-edit it.
- **Hooks cannot trigger `/rewind`** — but the existing **tmux send-keys** driver (`uucompactclaude`) can, by navigating the rewind menu.

The model Akien named: `load context` (once) → `bookmark base` (the post-/context-load point) → work the ticket → `clear to base` (rewind the ticket delta away, base never re-tokenized, commits intact) → next ticket. The win is paying for the static base **once** rather than re-loading it per ticket.

**Two native backends — only one built.** In-session `/rewind` (chosen, default) keeps it ONE session and fits the existing Granny send-keys dispatch model. **Fork-per-ticket** (`claude --resume <base> --fork-session`) was **deliberately deferred** (Akien): it spawns a CC process per ticket, colliding with the never-spawn-a-CC + one-CC-at-a-time guardrails (the 2026-06-01 runaway shape), for a benefit we can't yet articulate. Recorded here so the *why* survives — "they offer both for a reason; if we ever build both we'll know which to keep and why." For now: rewind only, no mode selector. The HIGH-inertia `m_devlab.py` migration and MEDIUM-inertia `cc_hook_pending.py` notes from the original audit trail no longer apply to this ticket (the checkpoint-file/preserve-string surface is gone); they remain relevant only to T-why-sorter's migration.

### Deferred to NARRATIVE (not ticketed — needs Akien / monthly-Opus session)
- The two-axis intent graph schema/IR itself (grow from seams, don't bless top-down).
- The generalized sequence designer riding on the IR.
- **CC.0-discuss / CC.1-build role split.** Exploration confirmed no production role-split exists (only `unseen_university/test_minions/cc1.py`, a test harness). Large surface — Granny dispatch + availability gates + daemon arch; design-unknown. The **clear-to-base ticket (T-per-ticket-checkpoint, reframed) is the buildable substrate** the split rides on — CC.1 is "a builder session that clears to base between tickets." The role split (who/when/dispatch) still waits for design conversation; the per-ticket compaction *trigger policy* is part of that narrative, NOT the clear-to-base ticket (which builds the mechanism, not the trigger).
  - **Provisioning ceiling (Akien, 2026-06-16): CC.0 OK, CC.1 OK, CC.n NOT APPROVED.** The split is CC.0 (discuss) + exactly ONE builder (CC.1). Any CC.n (n≥2) — additional/parallel builders — is gated on Akien's explicit approval; the system may not self-provision a second builder. This is the provisioning-side guardrail reinforcing the one-CC-at-a-time concurrency limit (2026-06-01 runaway), and the deeper reason fork-per-ticket was deferred — forking was a path to multiplying builders.

### Cadence note
Akien: "I should just do these conversations with opus once a month. This is more effective than the existing audits." Audits = horizontal drift detectors (bottom of stack); the monthly Opus session is generative, vertical, top-of-stack — the missing cadence above audits.

### Builder orchestration & cost-awareness (2026-06-16, Akien)
Employment order for the 3 builders (within CC.0 OK / CC.1 OK / CC.n NOT APPROVED):
- **CC.0 → designer/orchestrator** once DS is promoted.
- **DS.0 → primary builder**, GATED on getting DS up to CC level: validate its tickets + trust-track whether it really does what it's supposed to (ongoing, not a one-time check).
- **CC.1 → background/overflow builder**, reach-for-anything. Runs when DS is busy / low on usage room, or there's a backlog AND Anthropic headroom. The ONLY "don't use CC.1" rule: usage is dropping too fast. Orchestration of Claude-only minions left to CC's judgment. Refinement: **rewind minions BEFORE a task, not after** — pre-task clear-to-base doesn't trust that the previous task ended gracefully (a crash can't leak cruft forward). So `clear_to_base` is the FIRST step of the builder loop.

**The cost-awareness intention (Akien's words):** *"I intend that each builder should be concerned about not starting things it can't finish and being aware that inference is a shared resource."* It is deliberately dense — a good intention compiles downward. It compiles into TWO sub-intents sharing one substrate:
- *local* — "not start what it can't finish" → a **pre-claim gate** (ticket estimate ≤ my remaining headroom + margin) → needs a per-ticket cost ESTIMATE + own budget-remaining.
- *global* — "inference is a shared resource" → a **scheduler + burn-rate circuit breaker** (DS primary → CC.1 overflow → pause CC.1 on fast burn) → needs a live SHARED burn view across builders/providers.
- **Shared substrate (critical path): per-ticket usage MEASUREMENT** — actuals attributed to a ticket id. We have budget/burn tools (`budget_remaining`, `openrouter_burn_rate`, `budget_summary`) but NOT per-ticket attribution. Estimator and gates are both downstream of it. → **T-per-ticket-usage-metrics** (the one buildable-now seam; everything else is gated on DS validation or on these metrics).

### Reflexive realization — the process IS the product (2026-06-16, Akien)
Akien, mid-session: *"I am deliberately compiling user intentions plus architectural intentions embodied in the system as a whole, into decisions and thence to tickets and thence to code. I just hadn't seen it that way before."*

The development process we are running IS the architecture-as-code compiler executed by hand. The compiler has **two joined input streams**, not one top-down stream:
1. **User intentions** — what Akien wants.
2. **Architectural intentions already embodied in the system** — what the code/constraints already are (surfaced when the compile pulls in existing infra, gaps, and rules as co-inputs).

They JOIN → **decision** → **tickets** → **code**. The monthly-Opus design cadence (above) is therefore not a meeting but a manual run of the exact pipeline the project automates (compiled inference / the two-axis intent graph). The process is the prototype of the product — which is why it feels recursive. Implication: the artifacts of these sessions (this decision doc, the compile tables) are training/spec data for the eventual automated compiler. Related goals: [[compiled inference vision]], the cognition pipeline spine above.

## Audit trail
- audit-design: PASS with one HIGH-inertia surface (m_devlab.py DB migration).
- advisor (L/XL review): ticket 1 fileable after 2 fixes (scope-narrow to presence-check; stamp HIGH-inertia migration). Ticket 2 split — checkpoint half buildable (M), CC.0/CC.1 → narrative. Both applied.
- audit-ticket: all three PASS. HIGH-inertia (m_devlab.py) + MEDIUM-inertia (cc_hook_pending.py compaction pipe) stamped in ticket bodies; gates fire at sprint/build time.
